Q: Built Quant from a large knowledge gap using ANA PREP material, especially for fundamentals.
Learned that rushing through all topics with only a few questions each caused later rework; recommended doing enough questions in one topic before moving on.
Practiced all difficulty levels instead of only 700+ questions, with particular emphasis on 550-650 level questions for efficiency and accuracy.
Focused heavily on percentages and ratios because they appeared repeatedly in Quant and DI.
For basic calculation mistakes, practiced many easy and medium GMAT Club questions under timed conditions while maintaining accuracy.
Used plugging values as a fast route on some hard algebra and remainder questions.
V: Improved Verbal timing by reading the question stem and answer choices clearly without getting distracted.
Trusted first reactions when the prompt and answer choices had been read carefully.
Avoided overcomplicating Verbal with study materials that felt harder than the actual questions.
Used a TTP CR video shortly before the exam to codify a simple method for harder CR questions.
Benefited from a strong reading habit built outside GMAT prep.
DI: Treated DI timing as the main bottleneck after an initial low score.
Rejected the strategy of skipping MSR entirely because it lowered the score ceiling.
For MSR, read the sources once for a general map, then returned to the relevant information as each question required.
Applied a hard stop to TPA: if no clear approach appeared within about 1.5 minutes, guessed and moved on.
Used saved TPA time on Data Sufficiency, where accuracy was higher.
Focused on easier DI accuracy and time management as a path to an 80-level score.
overall: Skipped an initial diagnostic because early practice already showed a large knowledge gap and a mock would have been demoralizing.
Improved from a 485 mock to 695 official in roughly six weeks through course correction and targeted strategy.
Viewed the GMAT as a test of strategy as much as knowledge.
Used official questions and GMAT Club filters to target source and difficulty.
Took multiple mocks to build the stamina to sit and focus for the full exam.

Q: After Verbal and DI improved quickly, spent most remaining prep time on Quant.
Used practice exams to diagnose weak areas, then studied those areas before repeating the cycle.
overall: Prepared for about three months at roughly 15-20 hours per week.
Used the Official Guide and extra practice exams as the main prep base.
Used AI tools to explain official questions and generate related practice questions on the same concepts.
Checked AI-generated questions carefully because some had contradictions or unsolvable setups.
Improved prompt wording so AI-generated drills included GMAT-style answer choices and answerable questions.

Q: Focused on streaks and accuracy in the first 10 Quant questions, which produced the biggest score increase.
Used GMAT Club to become faster and more accurate on medium questions rather than focusing only on 700+ material.
Drilled weak Quant topics with Gemini, using it to identify deficient subareas and generate unlimited practice.
Practiced both 555-605 and 605-655 medium questions to build reliable execution.
DI: Applied the same early-streak and accuracy mindset to DI.
Recognized that the real exam may punish wrong answers more harshly than practice tests, so aimed for higher accuracy.
overall: After a 675 first attempt despite mid-700s practice tests, targeted execution rather than broad new content.
Handled test-day nerves by taking a short pause to calm down and reengage with the question.
Used free Gemini rather than a paid AI version.
Took the retake at a test center.

Q: Raised Quant from strong to Q90 by fixing behavioral issues rather than learning new math.
Stopped rushing easy questions and gave every Quant question the same level of focus.
Wrote out every algebra step instead of relying on mental math.
Highlighted key words such as must, could, and except before solving.
Used one Quant sectional every five to seven days to stay sharp.
V: Shifted from passive to active RC reading, focusing on the author’s purpose rather than memorizing details.
Practiced Verbal first under relaxed timing and then under standard timing.
Tracked patterns such as unnecessary rereading and rushing particular question types.
Reduced RC timing pressure enough to finish Verbal with a few minutes left.
DI: Tracked DI accuracy by subsection and discovered Data Sufficiency was the weakest area.
Allocated most DI prep time to DS rather than spending more time on already-strong areas.
Accepted that DI may still produce test-day curveballs even after strong practice performance.
overall: Moved from 665 to 715 across three attempts by improving structure, execution, and composure.
Replaced random videos and OG work with a more systematic prep approach.
After a disappointing second attempt, recognized that pressure and second-guessing had hurt Verbal.
Used mindfulness before mocks and treated final mocks as dress rehearsals for nerves.
Framed the 600s plateau as often an execution problem rather than a knowledge problem.

DI: Used GMAT Ninja videos and e-GMAT modules for DI, viewing DI as a combination of Quant and Verbal skills.
overall: Persisted through five attempts over three years and a transition from classic GMAT to Focus Edition.
Used detailed error logs to preserve formulas, strategies, and insights across long breaks.
Kept a separate sheet of winning habits and reviewed it daily until useful approaches became automatic.
After burnout and a seven-month break, returned for a no-regret attempt with lower outcome pressure.
Found that letting go of expectations helped test-day execution and pacing.

Q: Started with little math foundation and spent the first four months building Quant basics through TTP.
Used OG questions and mock tests for another phase of repeated practice after completing the Quant foundation.
Maintained a detailed error tracker after each mock, logging topic, time spent, difficulty, reason for the miss, next-step correction, and a GMAT Club link.
Tracked questions that took more than 2.5 minutes and redid them under timed conditions.
Read multiple GMAT Club explanations to compare solution paths and choose methods that fit best.
Returned to TTP hard tests for weak topics discovered during mocks instead of completing every chapter test indiscriminately.
V: Used TTP Critical Reasoning to learn a formulaic approach to each question type.
Relied on structured CR methods to maintain near-perfect Verbal performance.
Did not spend much time changing RC because it was already a natural strength.
DI: Tracked DI questions that took more than 2.5 minutes and redid them under timed conditions.
Used timing checkpoints, such as target times at questions 6, 12, and 17, to stay grounded during DI.
Treated DI timing as a major challenge and used checkpoints to prevent panic.
overall: Moved from 645 to 595 to 695 by combining structure, self-awareness, and persistence.
Used many mocks but spaced them out after overtesting before the second attempt caused burnout.
Before the final attempt, took only one or two mocks per week and none in the final four days.
Spent the final week reviewing the error tracker and used only a short warm-up quiz on test morning.
Accepted inconsistent mock scores and avoided panic when final practice results varied.
